original_hypothesis.title: AddressManager can brick critical protocol dependencies by setting named addresses to EOA/zero, causing hard reverts in core flows
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Core flows assume resolver returns valid contract addresses (bridge, tier_provider, taiko_token, signal_service, verifiers). AddressManager.setAddress allows owner to set any address, including zero or an EOA. This can cause downstream external calls to revert (e.g., calling getTier on an EOA), halting proposing/proving/verifying or misrouting ETH to bridge.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a configuration/ops integrity risk: a single misconfiguration (or malicious governance action) can stop the chain. Need to verify operational safeguards around address changes and whether contract-level validation exists elsewhere (not shown).
deployment_assumptions: AddressManager ownership is controlled by timelock/governance or an operator that can misconfigure
No additional validation layer prevents setting invalid addresses for critical names
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: AddressManager.setAddress has onlyOwner and no code/contract validation; DeployOnL1.s.sol transfers AddressManager ownership to timelock.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setAddress(...) external onlyOwner { __addresses[_chainId][_name] = _newAddress; } // no validation

preconditions: Attacker controls AddressManager owner (or can pass governance to do so), OR operator error occurs
Critical names are used by TaikoL1/Verifier/Hook flows via resolve(...) with allowZero depending on call site
Affected function is executed after misconfiguration

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Resolver is a single dependency injection point; no typed/contract checks for critical targets.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time an address is updated

operational_questions: Which resolved names are called with allowZero=false and will revert if unset?
Are there runbooks/tests before executing AddressManager changes (on staging forks)?
Is there monitoring for AddressSet events on critical names?
validation_requirements: Enumerate all resolve(name, allowZero) call sites and classify criticality
Simulate setting each critical name to EOA/zero and observe which core functions brick
Review timelock/governance procedures for address updates and emergency rollback

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain AddressManager owner capability (directly or via timelock execution)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Set a critical dependency name (e.g., tier_provider, bridge, taiko_token) to an invalid address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Trigger normal user workflows to observe reverts and liveness failure
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Optionally combine with upgrades to conceal or persist damage
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: High off-chain (privilege), low on-chain once owner

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High: can halt deposits, proposals, proofs, verification, or state syncing
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: requires correcting address mapping via owner/governance; may be delayed by timelock/pause states

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple address changes in short time window

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
